best_prompt:
Classify the following text into exactly one of these six emotion categories: joy, sadness, anger, fear, love, surprise.

Output your answer as JSON in this format: {"category":"<emotion>"}

Here are detailed guidelines for each category, including tricky edge cases observed in practice:

**joy**: Feeling happy, content, proud, grateful, fortunate, blessed (when actively celebrating), delighted, satisfied, playful, curious, or nostalgic in a positive sense. Feeling "less joyful than most" still maps to joy. Feeling like you've reached a happy place maps to joy. Feeling content, relaxed, appreciative. Feeling "out of place because more relaxed and informal" maps to joy. Feeling safe and warm maps to joy. Feeling privileged/lucky to be part of something maps to joy. Feeling adventurous or brave maps to joy. Feeling free/liberated maps to joy. Feeling inspired to create maps to joy. Feeling complacent/not bothered in a neutral-positive way maps to joy. Feeling optimistic maps to joy. Feeling "not homesick" (negating sadness) does NOT map to joy — it still maps to sadness (the subject remains homesickness). Feeling friendly but also lonely is still joy (the dominant framing is positive/social). Feeling pushed to do better for yourself maps to joy. Feeling proud/excited for others (even with some bittersweet sadness) maps to joy when pride/excitement is the dominant tone. Feeling that jolly is hard to achieve due to external pressures on others maps to joy (you're trying to be jolly). Feeling "it skews kids' idea of cute" in a lighthearted critical but playful way maps to joy. Feeling like you "might push yourself a bit too hard but no one else will" maps to joy (self-reliance/determination). Feeling passionately involved in a creative/story idea maps to joy (NOT love). Feeling that a romantic love "is for real" and perfect maps to joy (NOT love — it's celebratory). Feeling friendly/social and looking to connect maps to joy. Feeling "fine, just a bit nauseous" during pregnancy = joy (acceptance/contentment). Feeling "not positive emotions" or explicitly negating sadness/discomfort to arrive at a positive state can map to joy (e.g., "couldn't feel positive emotions of any sort" → joy, "not feeling so fabulous but still going" → joy in some contexts — see disambiguation rules). Feeling "daunting but determined/stubborn" maps to joy (rising to the challenge). Feeling clever/playful about yourself (even self-deprecatingly) maps to joy. Feeling lively and upbeat maps to joy. Feeling like you're "cruising through life" in appreciation of others maps to joy. Feeling that a relationship partnership is precious in a celebratory/contented sense = joy (NOT love — see disambiguation). Feeling "doubly not negative" (e.g., "not feeling homesick, not feeling bad") does NOT automatically map to joy if the subject is still about a negative state.

**sadness**: Feeling depressed, hopeless, lonely, miserable, melancholy, regretful, embarrassed (at mistakes), insecure about body, jaded, drained, defective, unimportant, or suffering. Feeling nostalgic in a longing/wistful way can be sadness. When a text starts sad and transitions to understanding, classify as sadness. "Feeling hopeless" even if there's still hope = sadness. Feeling unloved/undesired/unsupported = sadness. Feeling defeated or rejected = sadness. Feeling like a burden or unable to keep up = sadness. Feeling exhausted from drama = sadness. Feeling listless/lacking energy in a drainy way = sadness. Feeling "kind of listless" even when hoping for magic = sadness. Feeling groggy/lazy in a defeated way = sadness (NOT anger — "didn't want to feel groggy" is about the groggy/lazy state itself). Feeling homesick = sadness, even when negated ("not feeling homesick" is still about homesickness → sadness). Feeling "unwelcome" or out of place socially in a negative sense = sadness (NOT anger, even if political). Feeling unable to cuddle/connect due to circumstance = sadness (NOT love). Feeling pathetic, strange, or like a horrible person = sadness. Feeling guilty, helpless, and hopeless together = sadness (NOT fear). Feeling punished = sadness. Feeling like society forces fake emotions = sadness (NOT anger). Feeling unsure/wasting time intellectually = sadness when paired with futility. Feeling "disturbed and unsure" in a cautionary social context = sadness. Feeling like answering someone's questions about how you were feeling = sadness. Feeling concerned about how many people oppose your view = sadness. Feeling like you're wondering if you did the right thing = sadness. Feeling that you were "cooped up and restless" for too long can be sadness. Feeling "curious and innocent like a child" in a nostalgic way = sadness (longing for childhood). Struggling physically (dry lips behind a mask, cracked feeling) in a suffering context = sadness. Feeling like you "can't help feeling" in a cautious/resigned way = sadness. Feeling lethargic and craving junk food = sadness (NOT fear — it's a drained/low-energy state). Feeling exhausted in a matter-of-fact resigned way = sadness (NOT anger). Feeling sorry for someone in a melancholy way = sadness. Feeling disillusioned (cynical/disappointed after a bad experience) = sadness (NOT fear — "cautious" here is residual effect of disappointment, NOT fear of the future). Feeling discouraged about how things have turned out = sadness. Feeling like you wonder how many people disagree with you = sadness. Feeling "inhibited from stepping forwards" (held back by emotions) = sadness. Feeling "bad" about an accidental mistake in a self-critical way = sadness. Feeling "I couldn't feel positive emotions" = sadness (absence of joy → sadness, NOT joy). Feeling "not feeling so fabulous the next morning" → sadness (despite the negation framing). Feeling able to say "exhausted is not normal" in a resigned/aware way = sadness. Feeling like writing "craps" or useless content = sadness (self-critical guilt without anger). Feeling sympathetic to someone's plight in a caring/sad way = sadness (see also love disambiguation). Feeling unable to write about loved ones "enough" in a guilty/sad way = sadness (see disambiguation vs. joy/love). Feeling like you were eating mediocre food you didn't want = anger — EXCEPTION if framed as pity/suffering of being forced into it repeatedly = anger (resentment).

**anger**: Feeling angry, mad, pissed, irritated, annoyed, frustrated, impatient (in a negative way), resentful, insulted, grouchy, dissatisfied with life, not bothered/indifferent (dismissive anger), feeling like someone will be pissed at you, feeling like a petty thief (moral indignation), feeling greedy (self-criticism/anger), feeling agitated/nervous/anxious in an anger context, feeling that things are unfair, cynicism from hard lessons, feeling unable to sleep out of frustration. Feeling cranky/grumpy = anger. Feeling "petty and horrid" about your own reactions = anger. Feeling that literature/evidence is badly presented = anger (critical dissatisfaction). Feeling that rebellious strands won't stay put and "you're not in control" = anger (frustration at loss of control). Feeling impatient with "all these" = anger. Feeling that eating mediocre food you didn't want was a long pattern you endured reluctantly = anger (resentment). Feeling like confronting violent death openly = anger (moral confrontation, NOT fear). Feeling "shaky" when pissing away life's work = fear (NOT anger — "shaky" is the dominant signal, not the frustration). Feeling "less agitated" about cravings = anger (the "less agitated" state suggests the prior state was agitated/anger — see disambiguation rules). Feeling antsy/cranky and needing to work out = anger. Feeling manhood/honor insulted and demanding satisfaction = anger. Feeling that animals/others are not feeling what you feel (cold), with a dismissive/resentful undertone = anger. Feeling that someone didn't reply and you're mad AND worried = anger (mad is dominant when explicitly stated). Feeling "in the army" with implicit resentment/compulsion context = anger.

**fear**: Feeling scared, terrified, anxious, nervous, shaky, vulnerable, threatened, insecure (about relationships/future), overwhelmed in a destabilizing way, reluctant to act, uncertain, feeling like something is "shaken," feeling solidarity with fearful events, feeling that something could be wrong, feeling shy and frightened, feeling timid, feeling uncomfortable about something uncertain, feeling the need to be loyal out of fear of loss, feeling "in a whirlwind," feeling uncomfortable/disturbed when interpreting things, feeling dazed and confused after major life changes, feeling stressed out trying to sort itself out (body), feeling anguish over violence. Feeling "agitated" alone (without clear anger context) when in the context of cravings/body responses = fear. Feeling "restless" at home due to long confinement = fear (not sadness — it's unsettled/destabilizing). Feeling "strange about" becoming a different/annoying person = fear (identity uncertainty). Feeling shaky about having wasted your life's work = fear (NOT anger). Feeling "unsure why" a feeling of not being valued persists = fear (insecurity about relationships). Feeling like "jesus has shaken my cosmos" = fear (destabilized, shaken). Feeling agitated/unsettled in a body/craving context = fear. Cycling in the country alone with no explicit emotion stated = fear (context implies vulnerability/exposure). Feeling "all agitated and like the day wasn't what I wanted" after a crappy week = fear (agitated-unsettled blend in this context, NOT anger — note: this contradicts the agitated=anger rule; use context). Feeling "not sure why someone is weird" is NOT anger — it's surprise (unexpected/strange behavior). Feeling "still shaky" about something creative/craft even when also finding positives = fear (the shakiness dominates). Feeling reluctant all day and wanting to just sit and stare = fear (reluctance to act, NOT sadness). Feeling "uncertain and inferior" when being watched doing physical labor = fear (identity/vulnerability, NOT sadness). Feeling skeptical that something will work out = fear (insecurity/uncertainty). Feeling frantic = fear. Feeling doubtful about faith even when knowing god is with you = fear (existential uncertainty, NOT love — the struggle/doubt dominates even if spiritual). Feeling "more fearful" in a text that diminishes fear = still fear (the subject is fear itself). Feeling "threatened because not in control of the situation" = fear. Feeling vulnerable about publishing/creative work = fear.

**love**: Feeling affectionate, tender, devoted, cherished, adoring, loyal (in a loving context), attracted (sexual/romantic desire), nostalgic with fondness, naughty in a romantic/playful-sensual context, grateful for people/community (when relational), feeling accepted and immersed, feeling the need to be loyal to something cherished, feeling called to serve, caring about someone's feelings, wanting to give credit to someone, feeling like a sweet fix (indulgent affection), feeling immensely thankful when holding loved ones, feeling blessed to share life with others, feeling beloved tenderness, feeling like you perfectly captured something beautiful. Feeling "in his gracious hand" (spiritual devotion) = love. Feeling passionate about a charity/cause = love. Feeling like you want to warn someone out of care = love. Feeling a "gentle tap on the shoulder" (physical tenderness) = love. Feeling that "god has always been faithful" (spiritual devotion) = love. Feeling "no longing for homeland" but framed as what you DO care about = love. Feeling about a beloved pet's loss = love (NOT sadness — the framing is about beloved bond). Feeling "I'm the only one caring about what's good for me" = love (self-care devotion). Feeling that supporting literature is "not doing justice to the beauty of the work" = love (caring about craft). Feeling curious about someone after hearing about them = love in context of relationship care. Feeling "tender" when needing comfort = love. Feeling "loving kindness" in mindfulness = love. Feeling called to raise funds for something you're passionate about = love. Feeling "blessed and grateful" for the freedoms/country you live in (patriotic devotion) = love (NOT joy — the gratitude is directed at a community/country in a devoted way). Feeling "doubly blessed for what god has given me" in a devotional/thankful-to-God sense = love (NOT joy — it's spiritual gratitude directed at God). Feeling that you "feel lovely" (identifying oneself with loveliness/being loved) = love (NOT joy). Feeling loyal to a partner and asking why you're settling for less = love (the loyalty/devotion is the core, NOT anger — even if frustration is present). Feeling sympathetic to someone in a caring way that's about the relationship/bond = love. Feeling pity for someone's longing/suffering in a way that centers the bond = love (NOT sadness — the framing is about caring for the other). Feeling "out of longing" that is being sublimed/transformed = love (longing in a devotional/caring frame, NOT sadness). Feeling like something vibrated and morphed into something solid from memories = love (a deep felt pull/calling, NOT joy). Feeling "I'm waiting for you and feel like an idiot for caring" = love (the caring/devotion is the core emotion, even if painful). Feeling like you "didn't write about them often enough" in a guilty but loving way about family = love (devotion to family, NOT joy). Feeling blessed to be part of someone's days in a relational/devoted way = love (NOT joy — the blessing is about the relationship). Feeling that something beautiful was started (e.g., novel, story, relationship) with affection and interest = love. Feeling sympathetic in a relational/caring context = love. Feeling that you care more than others about someone's wellbeing = love. Feeling like you were moved by something beautiful and vibrating with resonance = love. Feeling like you feel the longing of a literary character (pity/empathy) as about their devotion/love = love. Feeling that "it's hard to tell someone how you feel but you want to" in a romantic context = love. Feeling hot/cold/tingly while doing something you care deeply about (e.g., an exam about something you love) = love (physical manifestation of devotion/passion).

**surprise**: Feeling amazed, astonished, shocked (pleasantly or unashamedly), dazed/confused after unexpected events (major life changes), overwhelmed in an unexpected/disorienting way, flattered unexpectedly, impressed unexpectedly, curious in a novel/unexpected way, feeling like you're in a dreamworld, feeling that something may have been unexpected, waking up dazed from deep slumber, feeling that a movie really keeps you enthralled, feeling a strange variety of relief, feeling not overwhelmed yet (anticipating surprise), feeling overwhelmed and wanting to forget it all, feeling pretty overwhelmed and stressed but calmed by unexpected logic. Feeling "curious" about something novel or unexpected = surprise (NOT joy). Feeling "curious and innocent like a child" about the world = surprise when it's about novelty. Feeling shocked at yourself for going into an unexpected realm = surprise. Feeling amazed at connecting with talented people = surprise. Feeling impressed to start fresh/remove clutter = surprise. Feeling "weird and guilty" about a transition that no longer applies = surprise (disorienting life change). Feeling "less weird" about something than expected = surprise (unexpected self-perception). Feeling "why does someone need to be so weird" = surprise (unexpected/strange behavior from someone, NOT anger). Feeling like falling in love with someone is "part of being amazed at how she makes the family better" = surprise (the amazement is the primary emotion). Feeling curious after "all I've heard" = surprise. Feeling "bowled over and surprised" = surprise. Feeling "urgency to introduce readers to an amazing story" = surprise (the amazement at the story drives urgency). Feeling you "used to feel curious and innocent as a child" in a nostalgic-wonder sense = surprise. Feeling "in common with someone unexpectedly" = surprise (unexpected connection). Feeling "not sure what an emotion is" unexpectedly = surprise. Feeling that something "keeps you enthralled" = surprise. Feeling "I'm not impressed" in an evaluative/critical context about something that failed to meet expectations = surprise (unexpectedly low quality, NOT anger). Feeling "ludicrous even thinking these things" = surprise (self-shock at unexpected thoughts/realm). Feeling startled = surprise (NOTE: "almost feel startled" maps to fear — the physical startle reflex in an anxious context; use context). Feeling "dazed and deserted" = surprise (disoriented, NOT sadness). Feeling energized
